video_title: Why Macro Investing Is Becoming More Systematic | Allocator | Ep.35
gist: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions) argues macro investing has shifted from concentrated discretionary bets (Soros/Druckenmiller era) to nimble, data-rich systematic strategies; he favors mostly-fundamental multi-model regime frameworks, sees a still-pro-risk but inflation-flagged environment with oil as the dominant risk factor, and views LLM/NLP on text as the current frontier of alpha and risk extraction.

insights.claim: The big concentrated macro opportunities of the 1990s (Soros, Druckenmiller, Robertson making large directional bets) have largely disappeared; the explosion of alternative data (GDPNow, web-scraped prices, geospatial, behavioral tracking) has forced macro investing to become far more systematic and nimble.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Edge in macro now accrues to firms with broad real-time data and disciplined process rather than to star discretionary callers; allocators should weight systematic macro and CTA-style managers accordingly.
insights.quote: the big opportunities that the the macro concentrated traders have had in the '9s really just don't exist today. You have to be much more nimble

insights.claim: The underlying drivers macro managers care about (inflation, growth) are unchanged since the 1990s, but the data used to proxy and measure them has exploded — replacing once-a-month/quarter government data with NLP monitoring of global news feeds and foreign-language sources in near real time.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Faster, higher-frequency proxies mean macro positioning can shift before official prints, compressing the window in which slower investors can react to data.

insights.claim: PGIM Quantitative Solutions runs ~$110bn, split roughly 50% systematic multi-asset and the remainder quant equity (benchmark-relative, typically 2-4% tracking error), with track records going back 25-40 years.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Sizes one of the longest-running systematic multi-asset shops; useful context for who is institutionalizing the systematic-macro shift.

insights.claim: PGIM Quant classifies the macro regime with a Gaussian mixture model that treats the economy as an unobservable state inferred from many data points, deliberately excluding market-based data because markets move faster than the real economy; this medium-term view currently reads moderately pro-risk.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Shows a concrete, replicable regime-detection method and signals current positioning bias (pro-risk) from a major systematic allocator.
insights.quote: a a Gaussian mixture model that basically views the world, views the economy as some sort of unobservable state

insights.claim: No single model should drive macro positioning; winning approaches combine multiple models (traditional inflation-high/rising quadrant views plus statistical methods), echoing how Kaggle competition winners are almost always multi-model ensembles.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Argues against betting on one regime classifier; allocators evaluating quant managers should probe for ensemble/multi-model robustness rather than a single signature model.
insights.quote: If you look at the the the winners of those projects... they're all multimodel

insights.claim: Roughly 80% of PGIM Quant's macro models are derived from fundamental data rather than pure price/statistical signals, specifically because fundamental grounding lets managers keep faith in a model during drawdowns instead of unplugging an unexplained black box.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Implies a fundamental tilt is the behavioral antidote to capitulation; a screen for whether a systematic manager will survive a bad stretch.
insights.quote: If you have a purely statistical model and it tells you to go long, you know, go long Europe and short the US... there's going to be a period of time when that doesn't work

insights.claim: The macro modeling problem has very few degrees of freedom, so models must be built with economic priors and insight-checking at construction time rather than by running regressions and dropping coefficients into a new model.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Explains why brute-force ML is risky in macro (few independent observations) and why prior-driven design matters when assessing a manager's process.
insights.quote: this is really true with the macro side because the degrees of freedom is very very very few

themes.theme: Inflation as the dominant risk factor

insights.claim: Since the start of Middle East military actions, markets have collapsed to essentially one principal component — oil up / inflation up / equities down (or vice versa) — so a single asset's morning move now telegraphs the rest of the market.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: When cross-asset correlation collapses to one factor, diversification quietly fails; watch oil as the lead indicator and treat equity-bond hedges as compromised.
insights.quote: there's been you know like there's been one principal component right it's been you know oil's up equity down or oil up inflation up equities down

insights.claim: Patterson uses a ~4% inflation rule of thumb: below 4% equities can still perform (with commodities gaining importance), above 4% is where material damage to both markets and fiscal policy starts; he does not expect the US to breach 4% in the near term.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete tripwire (4% CPI) for rotating defensiveness and adding commodity exposure; a level to monitor for the equity bull case breaking.
insights.quote: typically the rule of thumb is around 4%. If you get you know if inflation's under 4% you can still do okay with equities

insights.claim: Commodities are the 'forgotten stepchild of multi-asset' and the best liquid inflation hedge, performing especially in inflation shocks; PGIM Quant always carries some commodity exposure, with energy delivering huge YTD returns on geopolitical risk.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Argues for a structural commodity sleeve in multi-asset portfolios specifically to absorb inflation shocks that bonds and equities cannot.
insights.quote: Commodities if you ask me are I I kind of call them the the forgotten stepchild of multi-asset

insights.claim: Inflation risk has caused PGIM Quant to shrink active exposures via a short-term tactical overlay while remaining pro-risk overall; their base case is conditions persist 3-6 months and then stabilize rather than worsen ('not going back to where they were, but probably not going to get worse').
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Signals a measured de-risking (smaller active bets) rather than outright defensiveness — a template for trimming rather than exiting into elevated inflation.

insights.claim: Reports of 60/40's death are overstated; it still works most of the time and survives, but diversification can now be augmented in ways unavailable 30 years ago — institutional overlays adding convexity/downside protection, return stacking, and democratized derivative-income/derivative-protection products once exclusive to private banks.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Frames the practical menu (overlays, return stacking, defined-outcome products) for upgrading a 60/40 rather than abandoning it.

insights.claim: Institutions have over-loaded into private markets and now most commonly ask PGIM Quant to get them back to strategic allocation using liquid, disciplined, systematic exposure with some alpha — because privates can't be sold without accepting a material discount.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Identifies a real allocator pain point — illiquidity overhang from privates — and a growing demand for liquid completion/overlay mandates as the trade.
insights.quote: once you've got invested in them you largely can't trade them unless you're willing to accept a material discount

insights.claim: Most PGIM Quant multi-asset mandates are customized solutions to a specific client problem (e.g. realigning a publics/privates blend), not generic '60/40 plus overlay' recommendations; clients rarely arrive with a blank sheet asking 'what do you recommend?'
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Signals the business has shifted toward bespoke completion/solution mandates — relevant to how institutional allocation demand is being met.

insights.claim: Simply buying puts (or calls) for downside protection is expensive; PGIM Quant instead replaces some equity with longer-dated call exposure and harvests protection through rebalancing, supplemented by defensive-equity and sector-rotation strategies that add convexity as markets fall.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Concrete cost-efficient hedging recipe — the protection comes from rebalancing dynamics, not from paying outright option premium.
insights.quote: it's very expensive to just go out and buy a call or go out and buy a put

insights.claim: Long-volatility hedges are regime-dependent: they paid off spectacularly in COVID but offered little convexity in 2022, when equities fell while volatility stayed only moderately elevated and never spiked — a caution against assuming long-vol always protects.
insights.speaker: host (Alan Dunn)
insights.so_what: Grind-down bear markets (like 2022) defeat long-vol hedges; tail-protection design must account for the path of volatility, not just direction.

insights.claim: PGIM Quant's current ML focus is language models on text — evolving from bag-of-words to BERT/FinBERT sentiment to LLMs that ascertain market themes — which they describe as a rich source of both alpha and, more importantly, risk extraction across quant equity and multi-asset.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Pinpoints text/NLP as the live frontier of quant edge; FinBERT remains 'surprisingly good' for sentiment, so edge is in application not just model size.
insights.quote: so much of human knowledge is encoded in te in text

insights.claim: LLMs are effective for evaluative tasks like judging the tone of a Fed speaker or scoring text sentiment because they pattern-match speech against the past, but they remain next-token statistical predictors only as good as their training data — not a substitute for understanding.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Calibrates where LLMs add value (sentiment/tone extraction) versus where over-reliance is dangerous, guiding due diligence on 'AI-driven' quant claims.
insights.quote: all of these LLMs are basically trying to predict the next token that's all they're doing

insights.claim: PGIM Quant keeps a 'human in the loop' — portfolio managers review every trade to ensure consistency with investment philosophy — and prioritizes tracing positioning back to raw underlying data for transparency, rejecting unconstrained robo-trading.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: A governance template for systematic AI strategies; transparency/traceability is sold as a feature, useful when vetting black-box managers.
insights.quote: There's still what we call a human in the loop

insights.claim: Patterson is skeptical AI is yet generating profits: building/calibrating these models costs hundreds of thousands to millions in time, and he doubts anyone is really making money in the space yet despite high commercialization hopes.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: A cautionary read on AI monetization from a practitioner — relevant to the debate over whether the AI capex cycle gets rewarded.
insights.quote: I I don't know if anyone's really making money in this space yet

insights.claim: The worst thing in model building is optimizing in-sample performance; the real problem is out-of-sample ('what happens next'), so models need just enough complexity to represent the world's richness without becoming a 'loaded weapon.'
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: On the simplicity-vs-complexity quant debate, Patterson lands on parsimony-plus-judgment; a lens for evaluating whether a strategy's backtest is real edge or curve-fit.
insights.quote: the absolute worst thing you can do is is optimize in sample performance

insights.claim: PGIM Quant guards against overfitting by forcing researchers to argue what would kill their own research ('give me the scenario that this fails') and runs a humbling exercise freezing development-period parameters to compare them against true out-of-sample live performance — which is usually positive but less positive than the backtest suggested.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: A concrete, transferable research-governance practice (pre-mortem plus frozen-parameter live audit) that allocators can ask managers to demonstrate.
insights.quote: What I keep my question to them is always what do you have to do to kill this research?

insights.claim: Because policy and market responses are now far faster (SVB's overnight bank run, COVID's record-speed crash and rebound) than slower episodes like the GFC, signals must be designed up front with shorter parameters and kill-switches rather than waiting for failure.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Compressed reaction times mean panicking at the COVID bottom forfeited the rebound; tactical models need faster decay and pre-built shutoffs to avoid being whipsawed.
insights.quote: If you if you got if you panicked at the bottom of COVID you really missed out on the rebound

insights.claim: Forward curves are systematically poor predictors of future rates — historically they forecast hikes that never materialize or cuts that never happen — reinforcing a data-dependent, nimble stance over multi-month rate forecasts.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Don't trust market-implied rate paths as forecasts; position for revision rather than anchoring to the curve's projected hikes/cuts.
insights.quote: rarely is it accurate. I mean it often times they're forecasting hikes which then never materialize

insights.claim: Quant models aim for only a small edge — being right slightly more than 50% of the time (a Moneyball/Billy Beane 'get on first' analogy) — which compounds over time and produces a smoother ride by avoiding big bets; PGIM Quant rarely makes large 'grossly over/undervalued' calls.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Sets realistic expectations for systematic returns (many small wins, low single-bet risk) — useful when benchmarking a systematic manager's return profile.
insights.quote: we want to be right a little bit more than 50% of the time

insights.claim: Rising retail participation and US meme-stock 'pockets of hopes and dreams' create persistent short-run dislocations where market-neutral/long-short equity can be right long-term yet unable to hold the position short-term — but this irrationality is a feature creating opportunity, unlike the institution-dominated bond market.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Retail-driven equity inefficiency is the source of quant-equity alpha; the risk is the short-term 'steamroller,' so position sizing/timing matters more than the thesis being right.
insights.quote: in the US you know you get these little pockets of hopes and dreams

insights.claim: Today's mega-cap leaders are unlike the dot-com bubble because they have real cash flows and are profitable businesses (vs. 1999 valuations on clicks and eyeballs), so the open question is whether the AI capex cycle gets rewarded — something Patterson says investors will simply have to wait and see.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the AI-bubble comparison while flagging capex-payback as the real swing factor to monitor for the mega-cap trade.
insights.quote: the tech bubble was all about names that were not profitable... The mega cap names that are doing well today all have cash flows

insights.claim: Emerging markets are the most common incoming institutional question and PGIM Quant's flagged long-term strategic growth area — long ignored and out of favor, but now showing genuine change with attractive long-term GDP growth and profitability.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: A contrarian, under-owned strategic allocation idea backed by rising institutional interest — a candidate for long-term overweight.
insights.quote: most of the incoming questions we get are about emerging markets

insights.claim: The biggest longer-term unmodelable risk is the US fiscal situation (debt-to-GDP, unaddressed long-term policy) heading toward a 'tipping point' that neither party is tackling; the term premium swinging from significant to negative and now growing again encapsulates these deficit concerns, but the timing of the breaking point is unknowable.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: A slow-burning tail risk for long-duration Treasuries; watch a rising/expanding term premium as the market's signal that fiscal concerns are repricing.
insights.quote: at some point they are going to become painful enough. We are going to you know hit the so-called tipping point

insights.claim: There is a single dominant macro 'oil/inflation' factor right now but the broad economic data (forward rates, corporate profitability, employment) keeps PGIM Quant in a generally benign, pro-risk environment despite the US economy's surprising resilience and acknowledged risks on the horizon.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Net read from a major systematic allocator: stay pro-risk but trimmed, with oil/inflation as the factor that would flip the stance.

insights.claim: Patterson's top career advice for technical/quant professionals is to invest in communication (writing and speaking), build a network before you need it ('by the time you need it, it's too late'), and commit to continual learning — none of his roles came from blind applications, all from connections.
insights.speaker: George Patterson (CIO, PGIM Quantitative Solutions)
insights.so_what: Practical guidance for building a career in quant/systematic investing; signals that soft skills increasingly differentiate technical talent.
insights.quote: you need to build it when you don't need it. And because by the time you need it, it's too late to build it
